top_lines_snippet: PERFORMANCE REPORT B PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS 1. Delivering the Plan for the Future As outlined above, our approach to delivering the ambitions set out in the Plan for the Future is focussed on three pillars (People, Places and Pathways), along with key priorities highlighted by the Scottish Government. Our Annual Delivery Plan is our mechanism to set out the organisation’s priorities for change which the Board (via sub committees), and the Scottish Government seeks assurance on. The Delivery Plan acts as a contract between the NHS Board and the Scottish Government. A significant milestone in 2023-24 led to the consultation and endorsement of NHS Grampian’s Integrated Performance Assurance and Reporting Framework (IPARF) designed to create better alignment against the Plan for the Future and the Delivery Plan. The Framework describes the: - Principles for performance management and assurance - Values and behaviours underpinning the Framework, particularly the need to approach performance assurance with a spirit of enquiry and learning rather than to be punitive or seek to assign blame for under-performance. - Dual purpose of performance reporting within NHS Grampian; it is not only to provide assurance to the Board and Chief Executive Team whether targets are being met, it also helps internal teams regularly monitor and assess their progress, ensuring actions are effective and aligned with desired outcomes. This data-driven approach enables the identification of areas for improvement and helps justify the organisation's chosen priorities. - The provision of a tiered approach to performance reporting across NHS Grampian. Annual Delivery Plan 23/24: Overall Status of Q4 Milestones at 29th March 2024: Of the 101 Deliverables reported in Q4 Milestones set out in the Delivery Plan, by the end of March 2024, 30% are currently on track with 26% complete at the end of Quarter 4.

PERFORMANCE REPORT B PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS Summary of Key Risks/Issues Affecting Overall Delivery People: - 29% of 24 Deliverables reported in Q4 Milestones are on track, 37% complete and 13% reported as significant delay - Progress on areas such as o Colleague inclusivity o National pay reform o Recruitment o Putting People First - Areas significantly deviating from plan o Reduction in locum staff use o Development of values-based culture delays o Support for people managers Places: - 26% of 57 Deliverables reported in Q4 Milestones on track, 23% complete and 17% reported as significant delay - Progress on areas such as o Achieved savings target o GRACE & GEMINI projects o Health Equity Plan & Population Health approaches o Dr Gray’s Strategic Plan - Areas significantly deviating from plan: o Fleet CO 2 emissions o Baird and Anchor new build o Hospital@Home Pathways: - 40% of 20 Deliverables reported in Q4 Milestones on track, 20% complete and 15% reported as significant delay - Progress on areas such as o RACH capacity o Increased bed capacity o Child Oral Health & Vaccination - Areas significantly deviating from plan: o Delay in planned re-opening of Short Stay Theatre 1 o Dermatology & Endoscopy projects delayed due to funding issues

PERFORMANCE REPORT B PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS 2. Non-Financial Performance Summary 2.1 Key National Treatment Targets Our performance against the key national treatment targets is summarised below: Attendance at Accident & Emergency 98% of patients should wait no more than 4 hours from arrival to admission, discharge or transfer for accident and emergency treatment. Performance - Total A&E attendances in March 2024 were 10,878, an increase of 11.4% over March 2023. - ED attendance rates per 100,000 population in NHS Grampian remain consistently among the lowest in Scotland. - The percentage seen within 4 hours in an A&E department in March was 67.8% (65.2% for March 2023) compared to the Scotland wide rate of 67.4% (68% March 2023). - The number who waited over 4 hours in March 2024 was 3,478 of whom 370 waited over 12 hours, compared to 3,396 (250 over 12 hours) in March 2023.

PERFORMANCE REPORT B PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS The pressures on Emergency Departments (ED) during the year continue to be well publicised across the NHS in Scotland and the UK. Our local analysis suggests that the ED performance was acting as the bellwether of the pressures experienced right across the whole health and social care system with capacity and flow factors affecting each step in a patient’s pathway combining to manifest at the front door of the hospital affecting the 4-hour performance. Delayed Discharge Reduction in the number of patients awaiting discharge from hospital into a more appropriate care setting, once treatment is complete. Performance - There were 166 patients, of which 154 were more than 3 days delayed at the March 2024 census date (72 at March 2023, of which 70 were delayed more than 3 days) ▪ There was no additional funding from Scottish Government for winter 2023/24, unlike 2022/23 ▪ There have been improvements in recording delayed discharges during the year - Of the mainland Boards, NHS Grampian continues to have one of the lowest numbers of standard and code 9 delays per 100,000 population.

PERFORMANCE REPORT B PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS - Patients spent 5,106 days in hospital due to delays in discharge in Grampian in March 2024 (2,575 in March 2023). Treatment Time Guarantee 12-week in-patient / daycase Treatment Time Guarantee (TTG) and 12 weeks for first outpatient appointment. Performance - The tables below set out the waiting list size for the in-patient/daycase (TTG) and new outpatient (consultant-led) appointment waiting lists at quarter end - The in-patient/daycase (TTG) list size has been trending down through the year, to the lowest level since September 2022 - The new outpatient appointment (consultant-led) waiting list size increased through the first half of 2023/24, to its highest level at the end of October; there was then a decrease over the next 3 months, with a small increase through March. - The proportion of patients waiting over 12 weeks for in-patient/daycase treatment was 77% at the end of 2023/24 (78.2% at the end of 2022/23) - 54.1% of patients on the new outpatient list were waiting over 12 weeks at the end of 2023/24 (49.2% at 31 March 2023)

PERFORMANCE REPORT B PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS - Grampian has an established clinical prioritisation system (Elective Surgery Category System (ESCatS)) for planned care. This ensures that all urgent and priority patients are identified following determination of treatment and that patients are seen according to clinical need. - Capacity building has been targeted at urgent and priority patients (ESCatS 0 and 1 including cancer) and to address long waiting patients. Nevertheless, the waiting list size for all ESCatS 0 and 1 has increased during 2023/24.

PERFORMANCE REPORT B PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS - The number of patients waiting over 104 weeks has increased through the year, to 630 new outpatients (1.2% of list size) and 2,021 TTG patients (11.7% of list size) at the end of 2023/24; in comparison to 268 new outpatients (0.6% of list size) and 1,841 TTG patients (10.1% of list size) at the end of 2022/23. Cancer Cancer Access Times: 31 days from decision to treat (95%) and 62 days from urgent referral with suspicion of cancer (95%). Performance - Cancer performance against the 31-day standard has been below the 95% standard for each quarter of 2023/24. - The 62-day performance has decreased through 2023/24 and has not yet met the national standard. - An increased rate of both urgent suspected cancer (USC) referrals and backlog in Urology & Colorectal pathways continues to be seen in Grampian, mirrored by the overall national picture. A significant factor in the breach of patients is access to theatre capacity, which is also under pressure from the complications of long waiters presenting with more advanced stage of surgery as well as high acuity complex non-cancer surgery. - Performance against both targets is shown below.

PERFORMANCE REPORT B PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS Mental Health Services Child and Adolescent Mental Health Services (CAMHS): treatment within 18 weeks of referral (90%) Performance - CAMHS performance has been above the 90% target for 2023/24, with the exception of July and August 2023 - Completed waits within 18 weeks was 69.6% for July 2023. This was a decrease from 92.9% in June, due to these reports now including patients who had originally been filtered to the Neurodevelopmental (ND) Pilot, which has come to end. Any patients who had not yet been seen through the ND Pilot were being offered a ND Targeted Choice appointment to decrease waiting times for those entering the ND Pathway. Many of these patients were seen in the month of July.

PERFORMANCE REPORT B PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS Adult Psychological Therapies: treatment within 18 weeks of referral (90%) Performance - Psychological Therapies performance has not met the 90% target during 2023/24, however performance has been trending up over the period.

PERFORMANCE REPORT B PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS 2.2 Hospital Occupancy - Occupancy levels across hospital sites has continued at high levels through the year, reflecting the whole system pressures that have been experienced. The diagram below highlights the bed capacity pressures at ARI over the last year compared to previous years. Source: Tableau (IP Occupancy Summary by Ward). - Mental health inpatients services have continued to experience pressure during 2023/24. Persistent high levels of occupancy at RCH are shown below. Source: Tableau (Hospital Occupancy) Further details of the Board’s performance against priority performance measures agreed with the SGHSCD can be accessed on the Public Health Scotland website at the following link: NHS Performs

PERFORMANCE REPORT B PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS 3. Risk and Uncertainty To support the success and effectiveness of NHS Grampian’s service delivery and governance arrangements, an enterprise risk management approach is adopted, which seeks to uniformly identify, assess, and manage risk in a proactive manner. This approach is formalised within the NHS Grampian Risk Management Protocol. Below details risks contained within the Strategic Risk Register during 2023/2024, which outline the significant risk profile which is being managed as part of the delivery of the Board’s Objectives. Risk Appetite / Risk Risk Title Risk Tolerance Rating Thresholds The inability to deliver planned and unplanned care Outwith appetite may lead to worsening health in the population of Very High & outwith Grampian, potentially widening health inequalities tolerance Outwith appetite Inability to effectively maintain and invest in NHS Very High & outwith Grampian’s infrastructure tolerance Inability to achieve the aspirations set out in Plan Outwith appetite for the Future due to financial resource constraints High but within and inefficiencies tolerance Outwith appetite Deviation from recognised service standards of High but within practice and delivery tolerance Insufficient change and innovation to create a Outwith appetite system which can meet demand and delivery of High but within our strategic intent tolerance Outwith appetite Cybersecurity incident High but within tolerance Outwith appetite Worsening health in Grampian particularly in those High but within who experience multiple disadvantages tolerance Deteriorating Workforce Engagement High Within appetite During the 2023/24 there was a noted increase in the exposure of risk relating to the delivery of planned and unscheduled care services, the financial position and infrastructure across the estate, which has resulted in enhanced focus in the control environment relating to these areas. Risks that are assessed as being outwith the organisations agreed tolerance levels are subject to enhanced monitoring and review.

PERFORMANCE REPORT B PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS During 2023/2024 the Board continued to manage risk in a way that focused on the safe, effective and person-centred delivery of clinical services, and helped fulfil the key operational objectives set out in our Delivery Plan. The last year has also seen the development and implementation of the Board Committee Risk Process, which aligns the organisation’s most significant risk to the Chief Executive Team, the Board Committees, and the Board itself. The development of Strategic Risk within the Board continues, including the identification of new or emerging risks. The Board’s risk management processes are covered in more detail in the Governance Statement on Pages 56 to 58.

PERFORMANCE REPORT B PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS 3. Financial Performance and Position 3.1 Consolidated Accounts The Annual Accounts consolidate the results of Grampian Health Board (NHS Grampian), Grampian Health Board Endowment Funds (Operating as NHS Grampian Charity) (a registered Charity) and the three Integration Joint Boards (IJBs) (Aberdeen City, Aberdeenshire, and Moray). The basis of consolidation, explained in note 1 Accounting Policies on Page 97, is determined by the extent of control NHS Grampian can effectively exercise over each of its partner organisations. All Trustees of the NHS Grampian Charity are members of NHS Grampian. The Board therefore has majority control and, using the principles stipulated by IFRS 10, must consolidate, in full, the financial results of the NHS Grampian Charity into the main Board accounts. Voting membership of each of the IJBs is however split equally between members of NHS Grampian and members of each local council. The Board, therefore, has exactly half of the controlling interest in each IJB and, under IAS 28, is required to consolidate only this share of the net assets or net liabilities, as a Joint Venture, into the main Board accounts. The financial impact of consolidation is summarised below with further details set out below: Reported Net Consolidated Consolidated assets/ (net Net assets/ Net assets/ Partner Organisation (liabilities) (net (liabilities) (net (liabilities) 2023/24 2023/24 2022/23 £000’s £000’s £000’s NHS Grampian 645,802 645,802 513,009 NHS Grampian Charity 48,848 48,848 45,252 Aberdeen City IJB 9,835 4,918 13,573 8,465 1 Aberdeenshire IJB 0 0 Moray IJB 2,024 1,012 2,342 Consolidated Net Assets 706,509 700,580 582,641 1 The Net assets figure reported for Aberdeenshire IJB in 2022/23 were based on unaudited figures for the IJB. Consolidated Net assets have been amended to reflect the audited presentation, these figures were disclosed correctly on the Statement of Financial Position and the Statement of changes in Taxpayer Equity. 3.1.1 NHS Grampian Charity Fund Operating as NHS Grampian Charity (the Charity) The Charity had net assets of £48.848 million at 31 March 2024 (£45.252 million at 31 March 2023). Overall, the funds available to the charity increased by £3.596 million over the previous year (2022/23 decrease of £4.055 million). This increase is represented by a net gain on the valuation of investments of £3.049 million (2022/23 loss of £4.165 million)

PERFORMANCE REPORT B PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS The charity’s investment strategy targets investments to deliver sustainable long-term capital growth and consistent income generation, and income generation has been maintained during 2023/24. The national and international economic and political environment was extremely volatile during 2022-23 leading to a reduction in the value of the investment portfolio of circa 7% (£3.7M). Market conditions have been more favourable during 2023-24 with the result that the previous reduction in value has been almost reversed with the portfolio growing in value by just over £3.0 million throughout the year to a current level of £52.3 million. The Charity relies upon the generosity of patients, their relatives, and friends, who have experienced the care of NHS Grampian, and other donors who are generous in their support of the Charity. Total income for the year was £3.905 million (2022/23 £4.112 million) and this is analysed between donations, legacies, and income from investments in the chart below: NHS Grampian Charity Income analysis 1,816 1,609 1,539 1,509 £000's 789 674 634 570 569 401 188 263 255 0 0 OTHER DONATIONS NHS CHARITIES CAPITAL LEGACIES INVESTMENT TOGETHER FUNDRAISING 2023-24 2022/23 2021/22 Total expenditure for the year was £3.359 million (2022/23 £4.002 million), £2.738 million relating to grants for charitable activities (2022/23 £3.471 million), £0.419 million on staffing and support costs (2022/23 £0.334 million) and £0.202 million on investment management fees (2022/23 £0.197 million). The net effect was a surplus of income over expenditure for the year of £0.546 million The charity will continue to work closely with all relevant Health and related services, to ensure that available funds are utilised effectively, improving the health and wellbeing of patients and staff in line with the wishes of our donors. 3.1.2 Integration Joint Boards (IJBs) The three local Grampian IJB’s are key partners and enablers to the whole system co-

PERFORMANCE REPORT B PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS significant operational pressures during 2023/24 there was a continuing need to develop the model of delivery for many of the core services provided by each of the three IJB’s. All three IJB’s are continuing to experience underlying financial challenges, which reflect demographic and demand pressures resulting in higher numbers of people receiving support in the community. There continued to be a major focus on preventing admissions, supporting people in the community, and minimising the level of delayed discharges in Grampian hospitals. These efforts were underpinned by developments in digital innovations and the community engagement agenda across all three IJB’s. A key priority continues to be the development of whole system partnership arrangements to embed learning and transformational change into clinical strategy and core service provision moving forward. The IJBs faced operational pressures and due to the worsening financial position over the year, Moray and Aberdeenshire IJBs required an additional transfer of resource from NHS Grampian and their Council partner to achieve balance following depletion of IJB reserves. The level of IJB Reserves at the end of the 2023/24 has reduced significantly from those reported in 2022/23. This is due to the requirement by the Scottish Government that IJBs should use their earmarked Reserves to finance expenditure before receiving new funding allocations in 2023/24 and the requirement to apply Reserves to balance financial performance during 2023/24. The reported financial results for each of the IJBs is summarised below: 3.1.2.1 Aberdeen City IJB Aberdeen City IJB reported an overall deficit of £17.311 million for the year (2022/23 deficit £24.246 million), reducing the retained reserves carried forward to £9.835 million (2022/23 £27.146 million). This reflects: A general risk fund of £2.5 million (2022/23 £2.5 million) towards the impact of - unexpected events or emergencies; and Earmarked reserves of £7.334 million (2022/23 £24.646 million) due to the - allocation of funding to the IJB by the Scottish Government for a range of purposes including Mental Health Workers and Integration and Change. A copy of the 2023/24 Aberdeen City IJB annual report and accounts can be viewed at the following link 3.1.2.2 Aberdeenshire Integration Joint Board (IJB) Aberdeenshire IJB reported a deficit for the year of £16.926 million ( 2022/23 deficit £27.934 million) depleting the retained reserves carried forward to a nil balance of £0.0 million (2022/23 £16.929 million).

PERFORMANCE REPORT B PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS The use of reserves within 2023-24 was required to offset expenditure. The continuing pressures across the system in areas such as Care Management and Prescribing contributed to the deficit position for 2023-24. A copy of the 2023/24 Aberdeenshire IJB annual report and accounts can be viewed at the following link 3.1.2.3 Moray Integration Joint Board (IJB) Moray IJB reported a deficit for the year of £2.659 million (2022/23 deficit £12.338 million) decreasing the retained reserves carried forward to £2.024 million (2022/23 £4.683 million). The use of reserves within 2023-24 was required to offset expenditure. The reserves are as follows: Other Earmarked reserves of £2.024 million (2022/23 £4.683 million) due to the - allocation of funding to the IJB by the Scottish Government for a range of purposes. A copy of the 2023/24 Moray IJB annual report and accounts can be viewed at the following link

PERFORMANCE REPORT B PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS 3.2 NHS Grampian performance against statutory financial targets The results of each of the partner organisations, although consolidated with the main Board accounts for group accounting purposes, do not form part of the statutory financial target set for NHS Boards by the Scottish Government Health and Social Care Directorates (SGHSCD) and are, therefore, not taken into account when considering the Board’s in year financial performance. NHS Boards have the ability to manage small under/overspends (within 1% of resource) of an overall breakeven position across a three- year period (2023-26). The Scottish Government require NHS Boards to meet three key financial targets: ➢ Revenue Resource Limit (RRL) – a resource budget for ongoing activity; ➢ Capital Resource Limit (CRL) – a resource budget for net capital investment; and ➢ Cash Requirement – a financing requirement to fund the cash consequences of ongoing activity and net capital investment. Health Boards are required to contain their net expenditure within these limits, and report on any variation. NHS Grampian’s 2023/24 performance against these three key financial targets is shown in the table below: Statutory Financial Targets Limit as set Actual Variance by SGHSCD Outturn (Deficit)/Sur plus £000’s £000’s £000’s 1,440,632 1,440,535 97 Core Revenue Resource Limit 48,416 48,416 0 Non-Core Revenue Resource Limit 1,489,048 1,488,951 97 Total Revenue Resource Limit Core Capital Resource Limit 113,641 113,641 - Non-Core Capital Resource Limit 3,465 3,465 - Total Capital Resource Limit 117,106 117,106 - Cash Requirement 1,649,045 1,649,045 -

PERFORMANCE REPORT B PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS Memorandum for In Year Out-turn £000 Core Revenue Resource Variance Surplus in 2023/24 97 Financial flexibility: funding banked with/(provided by) -24,800 Scottish Government Underlying Deficit against Core Revenue Resource Limit -24,703 1.66% Further details on non-core elements of expenditure, typically comprising items of a technical accounting nature, can be found in the Summary of Resource Outturn. NHS Grampian was not able to set a balanced revenue budget for the 2023/24 financial year. The financial plan submitted to Scottish Government in March 2023 projected a £60.627 million deficit for the year after assuming savings achievement for the year of £16.557 million. Scottish Government confirmed additional funding for Health Boards in May which reduced the projected deficit to £42.936 million and further additional funding in February which has reduced the projected deficit to £25.103 million. The deficit highlights the level of financial challenge experienced during the year driven mainly by a combination of continuing service pressures and high levels of general inflation. Difficulties in recruitment and high demand for services have resulted in significant cost pressures in a number of areas, with the costs of supplementary staffing being a particular driver. Formal monthly reporting of the Board’s financial position to the Scottish Government commenced from quarter 1 with ongoing dialogue throughout the year relating to funding allocations and other shorter term cost control measures that could be implemented to mitigate the Boards financial position. In order to achieve in-year financial balance Scottish Government has provided the Board with financial support in the form of repayable brokerage of £24.800 million. The brokerage is repayable to the Scottish Government at the point in the future when the Board returns to financial balance. Without this additional support, the Board’s final outturn would have been an overspend of £24.703 million (equivalent to 1.66% of the Revenue Resource Limit). A surplus of £0.97 million against the Core Revenue Resource Limit is reported for 2023/24 reflecting this financial support. In November 2023, Scottish Government also informed NHS Grampian that the Board had been escalated from Stage 1 to Stage 2 of the NHS Scotland Support and Intervention Framework as a result of the Board’s financial position. This means that scheduled engagement and review of performance between the Scottish Government and the Board will continue through the usual channels. NHS Grampian submitted its 2024/25 Financial Plan to the Scottish Government in March 2024. The plan does not present a break-even position, recognising the significant work to

PERFORMANCE REPORT B PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS The Board expects to record a further revenue deficit in the 2024/25 financial year but is planning for the level of deficit to reduce over the following subsequent financial years as outlined in the Medium-Term Financial Plan. It recognises the significant work to be undertaken to bring the Board back into sustainable financial balance. The plan takes a risk-based approach recognising the need to identify and contain operational overspends without compromising on the level and quality of services NHS Grampian provides. Discussions will continue with the Scottish Government over the provision of further funding cover (known as “brokerage”) to support the projected revenue deficit. It is expected NHS Grampian will continue in operational existence for the foreseeable future, with no indication by Scottish Ministers of any intention for dissolution without transfer of services or functions to another entity with associated funding arrangements. 3.2.1 Delivering Sustainable Healthcare – Finding Balance This was the first time that NHS Grampian has overspent against its revenue budget and highlights the severe pressures that the health system faces in the North East of Scotland. A number of actions were taken to combat this overspend including the development in the year. These included amongst other measures: Development of an Integrated Performance Assurance Framework - Vacancy Controls - Review and control agency nursing and locum spend - Efficiency savings programme - Value and sustainability programme - 3.2.2 Efficiency Savings Efficiency savings of £17.52 million (2022/23 £10.2 million) were achieved against a target of £16.51 million (2022/23 £5.4 million) for NHS Grampian services. The scale of these savings was significantly higher than in the previous year and the delivery of these savings was supported by the development of an assurance and performance framework. A Programme Management Office was implemented to support this along with other elements of value and sustainability work. The main savings achieved were in the areas of agency spend including medical locum and nursing costs, hospital drug costs from switching from branded medicines to generic alternatives, procurement, cross boundary charges, energy efficiency and overtime costs.

PERFORMANCE REPORT B PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS Recurring savings amounted to £12.38 million (2022/23 £2.5 million) and non-recurring of £5.14 million (2022/23 £7.7 million). 3.2.3 Expenditure Analysis Net expenditure for the year was £1,565 million (2022/23 £1,409 million), an increase of £156 million from the prior year. The Consolidated Statement of Comprehensive Net Expenditure can be found on Page 93, a more detailed analysis of Expenditure in note 3 on Page 120 and a more detailed analysis of income in note 4 on Page 122. Staff Costs increased by £100.6 million due to the annual pay uplift (2022/23 £54.7 million) which was an average between 6-12% for Medical & Dental staff and 6.5% for Agenda for Change staff alongside national insurance increases. These were offset by a reduction in the accrual for untaken annual leave of £8.24 million (2022/23 £5.6 million) System pressures resulted in additional expenditure to support staffing levels with spend on agency locum costs increasing by £2.7 million, agency nursing costs by £2.7 million and £2 million increase in supplementary payments for non-compliant junior medical rotas. Drugs and Medical supplies increased by £28.3 million (£21 million 2022/23) due to price and volume increases. This impacted both the primary and secondary care sectors and reflected the volatility in the pricing of medicines in the year along with new treatment pathways. Medical specialties within secondary care were particularly impacted due to activity growth. Increases in expenditure was also experienced in the independent primary care sector of £11.8m due to increases in activity and uplifts to national contract values. Increases in other non-pay costs for the year of £7.5 million, reflecting increased spend on utilities, equipment, and service contracts. These were driven by increases in planned care activities and inflationary pressures throughout the year as a number of service contracts were retendered. Contributions to IJBs increased during the year by to £7.7 million as IJB reserves for Aberdeenshire and Moray IJBs were fully depleted which required additional funding to meet NHS Grampian’s share of the overspend against in their respective budgets for 2023/2024 The above increases in expenditure were partly offset by an increase in services commissioned through the Health Board by the Integration Joint Boards of £41.5 million This was offset by increases in income of £29.5 million including charges to other health bodies, income for treatment and donated assets to the Board. As well as decreases in the level of the cost of clinical negligence claims through the Clinical Negligence and Other Risks Indemnity scheme (CNORIS) of £1.9 million. The provision for impairment of receivables is quantified at £3.8 million (2022/23 £3.8 million) and is disclosed under trade and other receivables in note 9 on Page 129. The

PERFORMANCE REPORT B PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS provision is reviewed regularly by management considering the risk profile by class of debt. At the year end, the Board provided £65.6 million for legal obligations arising from clinical negligence and other employer liability claims (2022/23 £63.7 million). Details are provided in note 13 on Page 134. 3.2.4 Impact of IJBs on Health Board Accounts – pre-consolidation Each of the three IJBs in Grampian (Aberdeen City, Aberdeenshire and Moray) are established as a separate legal organisation under the terms of the Public Bodies (Joint Working) (Scotland) Act 2014 and have full delegation of functions and resources, acting as principal, to enable integration of primary and community health and social care services. Accordingly, the Health Board is required to reflect the contribution to IJB funding for devolved health services, and the subsequent commissioning income from the IJB for those services delivered by the Health Board, as a distinct and separate transaction from the operational expenditure incurred delivering those services. The consequence of this in the Health Board’s accounts, is income of £700.871 million (2022/23 £659.360 million), expenditure of £683.102 million (2022/23 £593.658 million) and a net retained reserve relating to IJB directed health services of £13.788 million (2022/23 £31.557 million). 1 The decrease in IJB retained reserves relates to overspending from core services and increases in pay and non-pay costs and actions taken by the Scottish Government to reduce the value of some in year funding allocations to IJBs by the value of reserves had at the start of the 2023/24 year. This is explained in more detail in section 1.1.2 above. The expenditure is included in note 3 on Page 120 and income in note 4 on Page 122. 1 The figures reported for income, expenditure and net retained reserve in 2022/23 were presented incorrectly. Income and expenditure and have been amended to reflect the correct presentation, these figures were disclosed correctly on note 3 and 4. The net retained reserve was based on an interim unaudited position and has amended to reflect the final audited position. 3.2.5 Property Valuation All property was revalued by the Valuation Office Agency (VOA), independent specialists in property valuation, on the basis of market value, depreciated replacement cost or existing use value as at 31 March 2024. The values were calculated in accordance with the Royal Institute of Chartered Surveyors Statement of Asset Valuation Practice and Guidance notes, subject to the special accounting practices of the NHS and applied to reflect local market conditions at 31 March 2024.

PERFORMANCE REPORT B PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS The effect of this revaluation on individual assets was some gained value while others were impaired. The impact of those assets subject to an upward movement in valuation was an increase in the revaluation reserve of £48.457 million and assets subject to a downwards valuation seeing a reduction in the revaluation reserve of £4.032 million. Write down of previous increases of £5.748 million also undertaken. Thus making the overall net impact on the revaluation reserve an increase of £38.677 million (2022/23 £10.065 million increase). The overall increase in property value reflects an increase in construction industry prices during the year, impacted by rising demand and a range of economic and geopolitical factors causing supply bottlenecks, labour shortages and increased materials costs. IAS 36 requires that impairments charged to the Statement of Comprehensive Net Expenditure (SOCNE) in prior years should be reversed to offset any subsequent increase in value relating to that particular asset. The net value of impairment reversal on those assets subject to an upward movement in valuation, was £0.728 million (2022/23 £0.657 million upward impairment) and this was credited to net operating expenditure within the Statement of Comprehensive Net Expenditure. Further information is available in the section on key sources of judgement, estimation, and uncertainty in note 1 accounting policies, on Page 97. 3.2.6 Pension Liabilities The accounting policy for pensions is provided in Note 1 to the Accounts and disclosure of the costs is shown within Note 19 and the Remuneration Report. 3.2.7 Infrastructure and Non-Current Assets The Board delivered a programme of infrastructure investment totalling £121.3 million during 2023/24. Within this, a total of £117.442 million of capital expenditure was incurred (see note 7d) and a further £3.87 million was classed as revenue expenditure in nature. The overall programme was resourced using a combination of capital funding from the SGHSCD (£113.6 million, see note 7d), income from donations (£1.25 million), financial transactions funding from the Scottish Government for GP sustainability Loans (£2.24 million), NBV benefit from disposal of equipment (£0.31 million) and various revenue funding sources (£3.87 million) Major investments during the year include: - Ongoing Construction of the Baird Family Hospital and the ANCHOR Cancer Centre (£95.9 million). The Baird Family Hospital will replace the existing maternity hospital, breast screening, gynaecology, and neo-natal facilities. The Anchor Cancer centre will be located adjacent to the existing radiotherapy building on the Aberdeen Royal Infirmary site. Forecast expenditure £277 million with expenditure to 31 March 2024 £246 million.

PERFORMANCE REPORT B PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS - £5.3 million on a wide range of Backlog Maintenance and Compliance with Statutory Standards, such as Asbestos removal, Masonry repairs, Heating Systems replacement, Electrical System upgrades, Fire Detection modernisation and Building Management System updating - £11.4 million on essential equipment replacement across clinical and support services and investment in recent technology to provide additional capacity in essential services such as cancer diagnosis and treatment. The most significant replacement programmes included: ▪ £1.6 million to upgrade the Hutchison MRI Scanner and Suite at Woodend Hospital; ▪ £1.3 million to complete the replacement of the NHS Grampian Wireless network across all sites. ▪ £0.8 million on a replacement CT Scanner for the Radiotherapy Service; ▪ £0.5m on a replacement for the O-arm for Neurosurgery; ▪ £7.2 million on other essential replacement equipment. - Other areas of investment included: ▪ £2.24 million on GP Sustainability Loans in line with the Scottish Government’s GP Premises protocol; ▪ £1.6 million on the Greenspace and Flood Prevention Project at Foresterhill; ▪ £0.5 million to relocate the GP Practice from Denburn Health Centre, secure the building and reconfigure Carden Health Centre to support the relocated Practice; ▪ £0.5 million on Electric Vehicle charging Infrastructure at Aberdeen Royal Infirmary, Woodend Hospital, Inverurie Hospital and Peterhead Hospital; ▪ £0.3 million to upgrade and extend the clinic at Braemar.

PERFORMANCE REPORT B PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS 4. Fraud, Bribery and Corruption NHS Grampian has a zero tolerance for fraud, bribery or corruption and has robust procedures in place, to reduce the likelihood of fraud occurring. These include an agreed policy on the Prevention, Detection and Investigation of Suspected Fraud, Theft and Corruption, Standing Financial Instructions incorporating Standards of Business Conduct and arrangements for the disclosure of information in the corporate register of interests, gifts, and hospitality. Staff are briefed regularly on counter fraud matters including the confidential routes that are available to report suspected fraud, bribery, or corruption. An awareness program was undertaken in the year focusing on promoting awareness on a number of areas as part of talent development for aspiring managers along with targeted development sessions to raise awareness on health sector fraud and general awareness for reporting concerns. NHS Grampian works closely with other organisations, including NHS Scotland Counter Fraud Services (CFS) and other government agencies to combat fraud. This includes participation in the bi-annual National Fraud Initiative which is a data matching exercise. The Board also takes part in a post payment patient exemption eligibility verification system, which covers all Family Health Service expenditure. 5. Payment Policy NHS Grampian is committed to supporting the Scottish Government in helping businesses during the current economic situation by paying bills more quickly. The intention is to achieve payment of all undisputed invoices within 10 working days, across all public bodies and NHS Grampian is actively working towards achievement of this accelerated payment target. - In 2023/24, the average credit taken was 14 days (2022/23 15 days). - In 2023/24 93% of invoices by value (2022/23 91%) and 90% of invoices by volume (2022/23 86%) were paid within 30 days. - In 2023/24 87% of invoices by value (2022/23 85%) and 79% of invoices by volume (2022/23 73%) were paid within 10 days. The small improvement in payment performance compared to the prior year reflects work to support the purchase to pay process during the year despite challenges around the impact of wider system pressures on support teams. 6. Social Matters NHS Grampian promotes equality and celebrates diversity both in the services we provide and within our organisation. We also take our duty to promote equality and diversity in the wider community very seriously. The challenge for the Board is to maintain the excellent progress achieved to date, taking forward work for all 9 “protected characteristics” which make up equality and diversity.

PERFORMANCE REPORT B PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS The “protected characteristics,” as defined by the Equality Act 2010 are: race, disability, age, sex (male or female), sexual orientation, gender reassignment, pregnancy and maternity, marriage and civil partnership and religion or belief. The Board has also embraced the requirements of the Equality Act (Specific Duties) (Scotland) Regulations 2012, as amended, and published reports including the following key updates on progress. A) NHS Grampian Equality Outcomes Update Report 2021 to 2025, as at February 2023 This Report provides a 2-year update on our progress to achieve the objectives set out in the 4-year Equality Outcomes Report. Further information on our progress update can be found at: https://www.nhsgrampian.org/about-us/equality-and-diversity/equality-outcomes/ b) NHS Grampian Equality and Diversity Workforce Monitoring Report 2023 This comprises an analysis covering all 9 “protected characteristics” of: o The ethnic makeup of the NHS Grampian workforce o Leavers and new starts o Training opportunities o Promotions o Information on the Gender balance for qualifying non-Executive Health Board posts. The NHS Grampian Workforce Monitoring Report can be found at: https://www.nhsgrampian.org/about-us/equality-and-diversity/workforce-monitoring- reports/ An NHS Grampian “Mainstreaming” Report April 2021 to March 2023 This details what NHS Grampian has done in the last 2 years to make equality and diversity an integral part of the way we function as an organisation. The involvement and consultation requirements for each report were met in full. The Reports are available on the NHS Grampian website, to facilitate public scrutiny, as required by law. NHS Grampian has also published a range of other non-Statutory Equality and Diversity Monitoring Reports on a wide range of topics. Equality and Diversity Staff Training During 2023/24, 1,429 NHS Grampian staff received Equality and Diversity Training by TEAMS at a level appropriate to their role in the organisation. All training is recorded in Turas Learn, a portfolio designed to record learning in the NHS in Scotland.

PERFORMANCE REPORT B PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS Human Trafficking Human Trafficking is the illegal trade of adults or children for the purposes of exploitation. It is a serious problem and staff are provided resources on how to recognise and respond to situations where a person may be suspected a victim of Human Trafficking Modern slavery and human trafficking have no place in our communities and staff play a significant role in alerting appropriate agencies and taking immediate action. Demographic Changes and Social Cohesion Grampian is one of the most attractive areas in Scotland for inward migration. The changing demography of Grampian is covered in all our training sessions. In recent years, special emphasis has been given to meeting the needs of New Syrian Scots who have settled in Grampian, Afghan Refugees, Ukrainian Refugees and more recently asylum seekers. A great deal of work has been ongoing to meet the needs of recent refugees and asylum seekers. We provided multi-lingual presentations in their hotels and have explained about NHS services and how to access them, facilitated by "face to face” interpreters and supported by translated materials. Appropriate information and healthcare support has also been provided to these groups, in co-operation with Health and Social Care Partnership (HSCP) and local authorities. Staff continue to use the “Health Passport” where non-English speaking patients can record details about their communication needs, general information about their health condition and contact details of health providers (e.g. GP, dentist etc.). Consultation and engagements with our local ethnic minority communities Since 2008, NHS Grampian has carried out at least 5 involvement and consultation events with our local ethnic communities every year. These have been carried out on a joint basis with the Grampian Regional Equality Council (GREC). NHS Grampian and GREC continue to work together to ensure ethnic communities can access key information on health issues including mental health and wellbeing, active lifestyle, screening and vaccination. Information has been made widely available in our main local ethnic community languages for -non-English speaking people. It has also been made widely available in many different formats to assist our local disability communities. NHS Services provide surveys and questionnaires in other languages which allows service users to share their healthcare experiences in their own languages. GREC works with NHS Grampian to reach our local ethnic communities. Racial equality work NHS Grampian has in place: - An Equality, Diversity and Human Rights Working Group who are responsible for

PERFORMANCE REPORT B PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS - A high level Anti-Racism Working Group Chaired by the NHS Grampian Interim Chief Executive - A Joint NHS Grampian/University of Aberdeen Working Group - A Staff Equalities Network The Equality, Diversity and Human Rights Working Group awareness raising campaign has included production of videos where staff share their experience of racism and anti- racism pop up banner and posters. The ant-racism badge “We all belong to one human race” was launched in March 2023. In October 2023, NHS Grampian celebrated its first Diversity Festival and launched the NHS Grampian Anti-Racism plan. NHS Grampian has a longstanding commitment to equality, diversity and inclusion. The event marks the progress of the organisation as a workplace and community where everyone feels supported, included and empowered to make their best contribution. The NHS Grampian Anti-Racism Plan 2023-28 can be found at: https://www.nhsgrampian.org/about-us/equality-and-diversity/staff-equalities- network/becoming-an-antiracist-health-board/ LGBTQ+ NHS Grampian has continued our exceptionally successful Rainbow Campaign. Over 5,000 staff now wear a Rainbow Lanyard, a Rainbow Badge or a Rainbow Pulley Type Lanyard. This is a visible symbol to patients and our staff that NHS Grampian is LGBTQ+ friendly. NHS Grampian continues to distribute the NHS Scotland Pride badge which promotes inclusion for LGBTQ+ people and makes the statement that there is no place for discrimination or harassment of any kind in NHS Scotland. A member of staff who wears the badge pledges to: - Being aware and responsive to issues faced by LGBT+ people accessing care - Be a friendly, listening ally who colleagues and service users can safely approach - Use inclusive language and respect identity We had a large presence at the Grampian Pride Parade in 2023 and will continue to take part in Grampian Pride. Disability NHS Grampian has funded qualified British Sign Language (BSL) interpreters to accompany Deaf people to attend Vaccination Centres for COVID-19 vaccinations, flu jabs and other vaccinations to provide BSL interpretation and moral support. PERFORMANCE REPORT B PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS In 2023/24, the NHS Grampian Disability Review Act Group received an allocation of £20,000 (including VAT) and completed disability access schemes in different locations across Grampian. More information on the above is available on the NHS Grampian website at this link: https://www.nhsgrampian.org/about-us/equality-and-diversity/ 7 Sustainability and the Environment The Climate Change (Scotland) Act 2009 originally set out measures adopted by the Scottish Government to reduce emissions in Scotland by at least 80% by 2050. The Climate Change (Emissions Reductions Targets) (Scotland) Act 2019 amended this longer-term target to net-zero by 2045, five years in advance of the rest of the UK. In 2020, The Climate Change (Scotland) Amendment order came into force to reflect this and now requires NHS Boards to report on their progress in delivering their emissions reduction targets. NHS Scotland as a public body must reduce the greenhouse gas emissions from its activities, the activities under its control and from the electricity, steam and heat purchased by it to net-zero by 2040 or earlier where possible. All NHS owned buildings must be heated from renewable sources by 2038 or earlier where possible. The Board undertook a Climate Change Risk Assessment in 2023, whereby elements around the impact of climate change were assessed to look at the locked in impacts which climate change will have and the associated financial requirements to mitigate these factors. NHS Grampian’s Climate Emergency & Sustainability Strategy: Reimagining the Health Service for People & Planet 2023-2028 aligns to this and was approved by the NHS Grampian Board in October 2023 following extensive engagement through workshops, seminars, subject matter experts and informed discussion with external and internal stakeholders. The Framework of this strategy focuses on four main themes: ▪ Delivery of Net-Zero ▪ Greening Health Systems; ▪ Greening Places & Communities; and ▪ Collaboration across local and national systems. These set out our approach to respond to climate change adaptation, deliver environmental sustainability and promote biodiversity action as part of NHS Grampian’s Plan for the Future. NHS Grampian has a responsibility for the environmental stewardship of its extensive estate and facilities. Tackling climate change also presents an opportunity to improve the health and wellbeing of our communities, to avoid health harms and to reduce health

PERFORMANCE REPORT B PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS All designated Major Players (of which NHS Grampian is one) are required to submit an annual Public Bodies Climate Change Duties report to the Sustainable Scotland Network detailing compliance with the climate change duties imposed by the Act and the Amendment order. The information returned by the Board is compiled into a national analysis report, published annually and superseding the prior requirement for public bodies to publish individual sustainability reports. Further information on the Scottish Government’s approach can be found in the Climate Change Plan 2018-2032 while national reports can be found at the following resource: https://sustainablescotlandnetwork.org/reports 8 Events after the end of the reporting period No events occurred after the end of the financial year and before the date of publication that will have a material impact on the content of these accounts as presented. ................................................................. toc_subsection: Consolidated Statement of Changes in Taxpayer’s Equity
top_lines_snippet: FINANCIAL STATEMENTS Note 16. LEASES A lease is a contract or part of a contract that conveys the right to use an asset for a period of time in exchange for consideration. These have been accounted under IFRS 16 leases which became effective across public sector from 1 April 2022. The following expedients or election that have been employed by NHS Grampian in applying IFRS 16. These include: ➢ the measurement requirements under IFRS 16 are not applied to leases with a term of 12 months or less ➢ the measurement requirements under IFRS 16 are not applied to leases where the underlying asset is of a low value which are identified as those assets of a value of less than £5,000, excluding any irrecoverable VAT. The entity is required to apply IFRS 16 to lease like arrangements entered into in which consideration exchanged is nil or nominal, therefore significantly below market value. These arrangements are described as peppercorn leases. Such arrangements are again required to meet the definition of a lease in every other respect prior to inclusion in the scope of IFRS 16. The accounting for peppercorn arrangements aligns to that identified for donated assets. Peppercorn leases are different in substance to arrangements in which consideration is below market value but not significantly below market value. 16.1 NHS Grampian as lessee NHS Grampian have lessee arrangement across four categories (i) 55 properties inclusive of land, with a closing right of use net book value of £20.3 million; (ii) 91 vehicles (pool) with a closing right of use netbook value of £0.8 million; (iii) 82 staff cars, where substantially all the economic benefit sits with NHS Grampian with a closing right of use net book value of £0.5 million and (iv) 86 items of equipment, the most material in relation to energy infrastructure arrangements, the closing right of use net book value of £15.2 million. At the commencement date for the leasing arrangement a lessee shall recognise a right of use asset and corresponding lease liability. Irrecoverable VAT is expensed in the period to which it relates and therefore not included in the measurement of the lease liability and consequently the value of the right of use asset. The incremental borrowing rate of 3.51% has been applied to the lease liabilities recognised in year. Where changes in future lease payments result from a change in an index or rate or rent review, the lease liabilities are re-measured using an unchanged discount rate. Where there is a change in a lease term or an option to purchase the underlying asset NHS Grampian applies a revised rate to the remaining lease liability.
